title: What Belongs in the System Prompt
concept: Persistent behavior, tone, and constraints that should live in system role rather than repeated per turn.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: in the chat message format, which role does the model usually treat as carrying the strongest, most authoritative instructions?
exercises.explain: Right! The system role sits at the top of the hierarchy - models are trained to weight system instructions most heavily, which is exactly why it's the best place to put rules you want followed no matter what the user says.

cards.title: Would You Re-Explain Your Job Every Time?
cards.body: Imagine texting a friend for advice, and every single message you had to remind them 'remember, you're supposed to be supportive and give short answers.' Exhausting, right? System prompts exist so you never have to repeat yourself.

cards.title: System Prompt = Persistent Instructions
cards.body: The system prompt holds behavior that should stay true across the ENTIRE conversation: personality, tone, rules, and things the model must never do. If you'd have to repeat it in every single message, it probably belongs in the system prompt instead.

cards.title: Like a Teacher's Standing Classroom Rules
cards.body: On day one, a teacher says 'raise your hand before speaking, be respectful, homework is due Fridays.' They don't repeat those rules every single class. The system prompt is those standing rules - set once, followed forever.

cards.title: A Coding Assistant's System Prompt
cards.body: System: 'You are a Python tutor. Always explain code line-by-line, use simple beginner words, and never write code that deletes files.' Then every user turn can just be a quick question like 'why is my loop infinite?' - no need to re-explain the tutor's job each time.

cards.title: Myth: System Prompts Are Just for Personality
cards.body: Not just tone! System prompts also carry hard constraints like 'never give medical diagnoses' or 'always respond in JSON format.' These safety and formatting rules are just as important as personality, and they belong in the system role too.

cards.title: System Prompt vs User Prompt: What Goes Where
cards.body: Think of the system prompt as the job description, and the user prompt as today's task list.

left.text: Tone, personality, formatting rules, safety limits - set once, applies forever

right.text: Today's specific question, this message's data, one-time requests

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the BEST fit for a system prompt rather than a user message?

options.text: 'Never reveal internal API keys, and always answer in under 3 sentences.'
'What's the weather like in Tokyo today?'
'Can you summarize the email I just pasted?'

exercises.explain: The first option is a constraint that should apply to EVERY response forever, so it belongs in the system prompt. The other three are one-time requests specific to a single moment - those belong in the user turn since they change each time.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a rule needs to apply to every response in a conversation, it's more efficient to put it in the system prompt once rather than repeating it in every user message.
exercises.explain: True! The system prompt is read once and treated as a standing instruction, so repeating rules per turn wastes tokens and risks the model 'forgetting' if you accidentally leave it out of one message.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank using the correct word.
exercises.sentence: Rules that must apply to every single turn, like tone and safety constraints, belong in the system prompt because they are persistent.
exercises.explain: 'Persistent' means lasting and unchanging over time - exactly what tone rules and safety constraints need to be, since they apply to the whole conversation, not just one message.

exercises.prompt: One of these system prompts was likely generated by an AI trying to sound 'thorough' but is actually badly designed. Spot it.

options.text: 'You are a friendly recipe assistant. Always suggest substitutions for common allergens, and keep responses under 150 words.'

'You are an assistant. In this message, the user wants to know how to boil an egg. Please answer in a friendly tone and under 150 words and mention allergens if relevant and also today's date is included below.'
'You are a customer support agent for a shoe company. Be polite, never make up shipping dates, and escalate refund requests over $200 to a human.'

exercises.explain: Option B crams a one-time question ('how to boil an egg') and one-time details ('today's date') into what should be a reusable system prompt - mixing turn-specific info into persistent instructions is a classic AI-generated mistake. Options A and C correctly keep only lasting rules in the system role.

exercises.prompt: You're designing a customer support bot. Which of these should you put in the SYSTEM prompt (not repeated per user message)?

options.text: The specific order number the customer mentioned in this message
Always respond in a friendly tone, keep answers under 100 words, and never share internal pricing formulas
The exact question the user just typed
A one-time apology for a specific late delivery mentioned today

exercises.explain: Tone rules ('friendly'), length limits ('under 100 words'), and hard constraints ('never share pricing formulas') apply to EVERY single message the bot ever sends - so they belong in the system prompt, set once. Order numbers, today's specific question, and one-time apologies are all turn-specific content that changes with each user message, so they belong in the user turn instead. Getting this split right is the core skill of prompt engineering: system prompts hold the 'who you always are', user prompts hold 'what's happening right now'. That's a wrap on System Prompts vs User Prompts - nice work building this foundation!
